texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Absorption in a packed column using monoethanolamine (MEA) or an amine equivalent is currently the preferred technology for CO2 separation from flue gas streams. This approach gives high rates of absorption, high capacities for CO2 absorption at low concentrations and low raw material costs (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). However, column separation is costly and it is likely that only incremental improvements in the mature process will be possible. Membrane gas absorption (MGA) is an emerging technology that will potentially reduce the cost of CO2 separation. CO2 is transferred from the flue gas stream through a microporous hydrophobic membrane into a chemical solvent. Membrane contactors allow a reduction by 4-5 times in equipment size compared with conventional packed columns (Falk-Pedersen et al., 2005). To optimise performance of membrane contactors, the solvent should not wet the pores of the membrane. This condition ensures that the membrane resistance remains negligible relative to that of the gas and liquid phases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of the presence of monoethanolamine (MEA) degradation products on membrane hollow fibers was investigated using untreated polypropylene (PP) as a model material. Common amine oxidative degradation products were added to MEA to simulate a degraded solution. The effect of these degradation products on the membrane gas absorption process using PP hollow fiber membrane was quantified. When PP membrane which has been exposed to amine degradation products is used in a membrane gas absorption contactor, the mass transfer rate of CO2 is reduced relative to the use of unexposed PP. It was found that the presence of oxalic acid reduced the mass transfer rate of CO2 in MEA most significantly followed by formic acid and then acetic acid. These acids are believed to adsorb into the PP, altering the surface properties and reducing the hydrophobicity of the membrane. This in turn increases the degree of wetting of the membrane pores. The membrane was characterized before and after use in a membrane gas absorption contactor containing degraded MEA solvent and studies showed that membrane pore wetting increased by 22-31% after 69h of use. SEM images and XPS spectra of exposed PP membrane indicate that wetting may be due to both morphological and chemical changes in the membrane due to contact with the solvent. This study highlights the need to consider reductions in the mass transfer rate of membrane gas absorption processes associated with inevitable changes in the solvent composition that comes with prolonged use.

texts.orig: Polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE) membranes are cheap and readily available and thus would be useful in an MGA service. However, several studies that have been carried out to determine the resistance of PP and PE fibers to water and amine solutions have reported an increase in the wettability of the membrane surfaces after exposure (Kamo et al., 1992; Barbe et al., 2000; Wang et al., 2004). On exposure to organic solvents, the pore size of PP and PE fibers has been found to increase by an amount that is a function of the surface tension of the solvent; solvents with higher surface tension affect membrane morphology to a greater degree (Kamo et al., 1992). A mechanism for this change has been suggested; the solvent forms a microscopic liquid bridge between fiber filaments and subsequent drying of the fibers results in these filaments being drawn closer together such that van der Waals forces act to maintain a more open pore structure. Wang et al. (2004) exposed PP membranes to DEA solution and observed that the pores became more elliptical in shape and some pore sizes increased while others reduced, increasing the pore size distribution of the membranes. Barbe et al. (2000) also observed a change in the membrane morphology when exposed to water, but attributed this change to the non-wetting intrusion of the water meniscus into some pores which acts to enlarge some of the pore entrances. texts.orig: One of the major problems associated with the use of amine solvents is that they undergo irreversible degradation in the presence of CO2 and other flue gas components (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Three types of degradation can occur when MEA is contacted with flue gas streams: carbamate polymerisation, thermal degradation and oxidative degradation (Strazisar et al., 2003).
texts.text: One of the major problems associated with the use of amine solvents is that they undergo irreversible degradation in the presence of CO2 and other flue gas components (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Three types of degradation can occur when MEA is contacted with flue gas streams: carbamate polymerisation, thermal degradation and oxidative degradation (Strazisar et al., 2003).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbamate polymerisation requires high temperatures and the presence of CO2 and is therefore likely to occur in the stripping process. It results in the production of high molecular weight degradation products. Thermal degradation requires temperatures in excess of 205 ◦ C and is therefore less of a concern; although heat stable salts can be formed via thermal degradation in the regenerator bottoms (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Oxidative degradation requires O2 which is present in abundance in flue gas streams and requires the replacement of about 2.2kg MEA per tonne of CO2 captured (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). It is likely to occur under normal absorption conditions. Oxidised fragments of the solvent such as organic acids and ammonia (and gaseous products) are produced which results in the most significant loss of free amine. Organic acids such as formic acid and acetic acid can react to form heat stable salts such as acetates, formates, glycolates and oxalates that may bind to free amine (Rooney et al., 1998; Chi and Rochelle, 2002; Dawodu and Meisen, 1996; Supap et al., 2006; Bello and Idem, 2005; Lawal and Idem, 2005). These salts are difficult to regenerate, have no capacity for CO2 absorption, lead to increased solvent viscosity, a loss of free amine, solvent foaming and fouling and cause corrosion to equipment. The currently preferred method of dealing with this loss is to separate the active amine from its degradation products using ion exchange, vacuum distillation or electrodialysis (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). However, degradation products such as heat stable salts are always present in ppm concentrations in commercial CO2 separation processes employing amine based solvent absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MGA may minimize the solvent oxidation rate because the gas and liquid phases are not in direct contact. However, it is also possible that the inevitable presence of degradation products will have an adverse effect on the membrane gas absorption process. Solvent degradation products may lead to a higher pressure drop across the membrane length (when a hollow fiber membrane configuration is used) and cause damage to the membrane because the solvent is in direct contact with the membrane cartridge. If the gas-side pressure remains constant, a higher pressure drop in the liquid will bring the transmembrane pressure difference (the pressure of the liquid over that of the gas) closer to the pressure at which liquid will penetrate the membrane, particularly at the fiber inlet. Further, any membrane damage may reduce the hydrophobicity of the membrane and thereby reduce the pressure at which liquid will penetrate the membrane pores. In combination, these two effects may lead to (or increase) membrane pore wetting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work we consider the effect that three common MEA degradation products have on the performance of a polypropylene membrane contactor. Oxalic, formic and acetic acid have been chosen because they are produced when MEA degrades (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). The characterisation of PP membrane after exposure to model degraded aqueous MEA solutions is performed. The CO2 mass transfer rate is measured using hollow fiber PP membrane contactors and different degraded MEA solutions. Finally, these hollow fiber experiments are modelled in order to predict the degree of membrane wetting that occurs.
texts.text: In this work we consider the effect that three common MEA degradation products have on the performance of a polypropylene membrane contactor. Oxalic, formic and acetic acid have been chosen because they are produced when MEA degrades (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). The characterisation of PP membrane after exposure to model degraded aqueous MEA solutions is performed. The CO2 mass transfer rate is measured using hollow fiber PP membrane contactors and different degraded MEA solutions. Finally, these hollow fiber experiments are modelled in order to predict the degree of membrane wetting that occurs.

texts.orig: An implicit numerical method was used to solve the model using Matlab 6.5 /H17015 modelling software. The calculated concentration profiles of free CO2 and unreacted MEA could be integrated across the fiber radius to obtain average concentrations at the fiber exit. By determining the average number of moles of reacted MEA exiting the contactor, the average bound CO2 concentration could be calculated. Thus, the average CO2 flux or overall mass transfer coefficient could be calculated as required. A good agreement between the theoretical model and the experimental data under different experimental conditions was obtained by determining the proportion of pore wetting, w that minimized the sum of the squares of the errors.

texts.orig: Since the wetting properties of a solvent are a function of its surface tension, the surface tension of aqueous MEA solutions was measured using the pendant drop method. A modified pendant drop method was used. A clear cuvette was filled with aqueous MEA solution in which an upturned metal needle with a horizontal flat tip was immersed. A bubble was formed at the end of the needle fed by a syringe, which was controlled by a syringe pump. This equipment was interfaced to FTÅ200 software (First Ten Ångstroms Inc., Software Version 2.0 Build Number 187, UK) which allows careful control of the pump such that the bubble remains attached to the needle. From a series of still shots of the bubble, the FTÅ200 software calculates the liquid surface tension, /afii9828 lg .

texts.orig: XPS was performed using an Axis Ultra spectrometer (Kratos Analytical, UK) equipped with a monochromatised X-ray source (Al K /afii9825 , h /afii9840 = 1486.6eV) operating at 150W (15kV and 10mA) in order to determine the chemical composition of the flat sheet membrane surfaces. The spectrometer energy scale was calibrated using the Au 4f7/2 photoelectron peak at a binding energy of 83.98eV. Survey scan spectra were acquired from 0 to 1100eV binding energy and a pass energy of 160eV and region spectra were scanned at a pass energy of 20eV to obtain higher resolution spectra. The analysis area was 700 /afii9839 m × 300 /afii9839 m. The peaks were fitted with synthetic Gaussian-Lorentzian components and were quantified using the sensitivity factors for the Kratos instrument. The spectra were calibrated to the C 1 s peak at 285.0 eV and peaks were identified according to their binding energies (Moulder et al., 1992, Beamson and Briggs, 1992). Polymer samples required no pre-treatment, but were mounted in a copper holder to prevent them from charging.

However, beyond 2 days, the contact angle for the PP membrane remains stable which suggests that the PP membrane is degraded by the solvent very quickly and that further degradation is either very slow or does not occur. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PP membrane suffers extensive degradation on contact with 20wt% MEA for a period of 25 days. The pores appear larger and disrupted and a change in porosity is also apparent. Similar changes are also evident from just 2 days of MEA contact. Various mechanisms for the degradation of the PP membranes are possible. Studies have found that solvents can act to increase the pore size distribution and reduce the porosity of membranes by either intruding into the larger pores and displacing the pore walls or by causing the pore structure to contract as the wet membrane fibers dry (Kosaraju et al., 2004; Kumar et al., 2002). This mechanism alone is unlikely to be causing the substantial morphological changes evident in Fig. 3. The MEA could be swelling or adsorbing into the PP or alternatively, it could be reacting with the PP.

texts.orig: This oxygen increase could be due to oxidation of the PP membrane due to direct contact with a CO2/air stream or chemical reaction between the membrane and MEA and the MEA degradation products. However, the fact that the oxygen concentration varies in all four cases along with the nitrogen concentration suggests that the MEA and its degradation products are interacting with the PP. It is likely that the hydrophobicity of the PP hollow fiber membrane contacted with uncontaminated MEA is the highest (followed by MEA containing acetic acid, MEA containing oxalic acid and then MEA containing formic acid) due to these chemical reactions. When used in a MGA contactor for CO2 absorption, the performance of PP membrane is likely to drop at a faster rate for degraded MEA solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It has been established that the presence of MEA degradation products acts to reduce the hydrophobicity of PP membrane because these reagents chemically react with the PP. Here, the absorption performance of PP is examined when 20wt% MEA solvent contains either 1000ppm oxalic, 100ppm acetic or 1000ppm formic acid. In addition, the change in absorption performance over a period of 69 h has been considered. Fig. 5 shows the difference in the CO2 flux for both pure and degraded MEA solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As expected from the membrane characterization study conducted in Section 4.1, the presence of each degradation product causes the mass transfer rate to be reduced relative to the case of pure 20wt% MEA. Since each solvent mixture contains the same number of moles of MEA to react with CO2, it is likely that the drop in mass transfer performance is caused by an increase in the membrane mass transfer resistance due to the PP membrane being degraded or fouled by the acids. texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aqueous MEA solvent was contacted with PP membrane to confirm that destructive morphological changes occur to the PP membrane surface, which reduces the membrane's hydrophobicity (a drop in contact angle of approximately 10 ◦ was measured). Common amine oxidative degradation products (oxalic, formic and acetic acid) were then added to MEA to simulate a degraded solution. The effect of these degradation products on the membrane gas absorption process using PP hollow fiber membrane was quantified.
texts.text: Aqueous MEA solvent was contacted with PP membrane to confirm that destructive morphological changes occur to the PP membrane surface, which reduces the membrane's hydrophobicity (a drop in contact angle of approximately 10 ◦ was measured). Common amine oxidative degradation products (oxalic, formic and acetic acid) were then added to MEA to simulate a degraded solution. The effect of these degradation products on the membrane gas absorption process using PP hollow fiber membrane was quantified.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Contact of the three common MEA degradation products was confirmed to change the chemical composition of the membrane surface, presumably through chemical reactions with the PP. The mass concentration of oxygen on the surface increased by approximately 10% after exposure to MEA contaminated with formic acid. A reduced contact angle was measured on PP membrane of up to 5 ◦ using the degraded aqueous MEA solutions relative to un-degraded MEA. Exposure of PP membrane to degraded MEA solutions also caused a reduction in contact angle relative to the case where PP membrane was exposed to un-degraded MEA.
texts.text: Contact of the three common MEA degradation products was confirmed to change the chemical composition of the membrane surface, presumably through chemical reactions with the PP. The mass concentration of oxygen on the surface increased by approximately 10% after exposure to MEA contaminated with formic acid. A reduced contact angle was measured on PP membrane of up to 5 ◦ using the degraded aqueous MEA solutions relative to un-degraded MEA. Exposure of PP membrane to degraded MEA solutions also caused a reduction in contact angle relative to the case where PP membrane was exposed to un-degraded MEA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The degradation products were also found to reduce the CO2 absorption rate over time below that of a system operating with uncontaminated MEA solvent, presumably due to the degradation products reacting with the PP surface and changing the chemical composition of the PP membrane surface, which in turn reduces membrane hydrophobicity. The results indicate that the PP fibers used with undegraded 20wt% MEA show a 22% reduction in mass transfer rate after 69h, while those used with model degraded MEA solutions show a reduction in mass transfer rate of 22-31% in the same time period. Preliminary work on improving the hydrophobicity of flat sheet PP membrane is promising (Franco et al., 2008b) but is yet to be adapted for hollow fibers.

Preliminary work on improving the hydrophobicity of flat sheet PP membrane is promising (Franco et al., 2008b) but is yet to be adapted for hollow fibers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is important to consider the effect that non-ideal solvent solutions will have on the CO2 absorption efficiency through different membrane surfaces. Furthermore, the effect of solvent impurities should be considered in the design of MGA systems.
